1:
"Now Peter and John went up together into the temple at the hour of prayer, being the ninth hour." The witnesses note the harmony of the two apostles and their going to the temple at the appointed hour. Chrysostom observes the constant fellowship of Peter and John, "everywhere in great harmony together" (as at the supper, the tomb, John 21); and notes that the writer omits the other miracles but records this one, "by which they were all put in commotion." He asks why they went to the temple — "did they still live as Jews? No, but for expediency" — using the hour of prayer as an occasion for ministry. The witnesses note the apostles going up at the ninth hour, the time of the evening sacrifice and prayer. The witnesses note Peter and John together at the temple.

The witnesses note Peter and John going to the temple at the hour of prayer — the apostles in harmony, seizing the occasion.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' going to the temple at the hour of prayer. The lesson the expositors draw is the harmony of the apostles, their use of the appointed times of devotion, and the providence that set the stage for the miracle. The witnesses note the pair: "Peter and John" — Chrysostom marks their constant fellowship, found everywhere together in the Gospels (at the supper, the tomb, the sea); the two leading apostles united in their ministry, an example of the fellowship and partnership of Christ's servants. The expositors observe the time: "the hour of prayer, being the ninth hour" — three in the afternoon, the time of the evening sacrifice and the public prayer at the temple; the apostles going up to join in the appointed devotions. The witnesses note Chrysostom's question whether the apostles still lived as Jews: not that they were bound by the temple-worship, the old order being fulfilled in Christ; but they used the hour of prayer and the gathering at the temple as an occasion for ministry — meeting the people where they gathered, that they might preach Christ and (here) work the miracle that would draw the multitude. The expositors draw the lesson on the use of occasions: the apostles seized the opportunity of the temple-gathering to advance the gospel; the believer is to use the times and places of devotion and concourse as occasions for witness and good. The witnesses observe the providence in the timing: the apostles' going at that hour, by that gate, brought them to the lame man — the meeting no accident, but the ordering of God, setting the stage for the miracle and the sermon that would follow. The expositors note the modesty of the apostles, which Chrysostom marks: they did not go seeking to display their power, but went up to pray; the miracle came unsought, in the way of their devotion — free of ambition, imitating their Master. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the harmony of the apostles, the use of the hour of prayer, and the providence that orders the occasions of ministry. The verse thus records Peter and John going to the temple at the hour of prayer — teaching the harmony of the apostles, the use of the appointed times of devotion, and the providence that set the stage for the great miracle.
2:
"And a certain man lame from his mother's womb was carried, whom they laid daily at the gate of the temple which is called Beautiful, to ask alms of them that entered into the temple;" The witnesses note the lame man's lifelong affliction and his place at the temple gate. Gill stresses that the man "was born so; his lameness came not through any disease or fall... but from a defect in nature," which made the miracle "the more extraordinary." Lightfoot discusses the identity of "the gate called Beautiful." The witnesses note the man, lame from birth, laid daily at the Beautiful gate to beg alms of the temple-goers — his condition lifelong, well-known, and incurable. The witnesses note the helpless beggar at the gate.

The witnesses note the man lame from birth — laid daily to beg at the temple gate, his affliction lifelong and incurable.

So the witnesses make the verse the lame man's affliction and his place at the gate. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth and notoriety of his helplessness, which heightened the wonder of the miracle and prefigured the helplessness of the sinner. The witnesses note the lifelong nature of his affliction: "lame from his mother's womb" — Gill stresses that he was born lame, his condition no result of accident or disease, but a defect in nature; and (as the next chapter notes, Acts 4:22) he was over forty years old, having never walked. The expositors press the significance of this: the miracle was the more extraordinary because the lameness was congenital, lifelong, and beyond all cure — no art of medicine had helped him in forty years; the cure, when it came, was manifestly the work of God, not of nature or skill. The witnesses note his place and practice: "laid daily at the gate of the temple which is called Beautiful, to ask alms" — carried each day to the temple gate (Lightfoot discusses which gate the "Beautiful" was) to beg of those entering; his helplessness reducing him to dependence on the charity of the worshippers. The expositors draw the lesson on the notoriety of his condition: laid there daily for years, he was well-known to all the people, who had often seen him and given him alms; so that, when he was healed, none could doubt the reality of the cure — the man's lifelong lameness being public knowledge. The witnesses observe the spiritual figure many draw: the lame man, helpless from birth, unable to walk or come to God by his own power, begging at the gate of the temple, is an image of the sinner — born in spiritual impotence, unable to walk in God's ways, lying outside, dependent on grace; the cure a picture of the saving power that gives strength to the helpless. The expositors note the providence in his being there: laid at that gate, on that day, at that hour, he met the apostles — the meeting ordained for his healing and for the glory of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the depth of the man's helplessness, the notoriety that confirmed the miracle, and the figure of the sinner's impotence and the grace that heals. The verse thus records the lame man at the Beautiful gate — lame from birth, begging daily — teaching the depth of his helplessness, the notoriety that heightened the miracle, and the figure of the sinner saved by grace.
3:
"Who seeing Peter and John about to go into the temple asked an alms." The witnesses note the beggar's request and the unexpected greater gift. Barnes notes "there is no evidence that he was acquainted with them, or knew who they were; he asked of them as he was accustomed to do of the multitude." Calvin draws the lesson of God's surpassing grace: "we see how God restored this lame man to his limbs contrary to his expectation; because he thought his disease incurable, he was only careful for maintenance" — yet "that is given him which he durst never have asked"; so God often prevents (goes before) us, giving more than we seek. The witnesses note the man asking only alms, while God purposed his healing. The witnesses note the beggar's small request.

The witnesses note the beggar asking only alms — while God purposed to give him far more than he sought.

So the witnesses make the verse the beggar's request and the greater gift God purposed. The lesson the expositors draw is the surpassing grace of God, who gives more than we ask or expect. The witnesses note the request: "seeing Peter and John about to go into the temple [he] asked an alms" — the beggar, as was his daily custom, asked the entering apostles for money; Barnes notes he did not know them, but asked as he did of any of the multitude; his request the small, accustomed plea for alms. The expositors press Calvin's lesson on the disproportion between what he asked and what he received: the man, thinking his lameness incurable, was careful only for his maintenance, asking but a coin to live; yet God purposed to give him what he never dreamed of asking — the healing of his lifelong affliction; "that is given him which he durst never have asked." The witnesses draw out the doctrine of God's preventing grace: God often goes before us, anticipating our desires, giving more than we seek or even know to seek; he does not wait to be urged, but bestows beyond our asking — the lame man seeking alms, receiving health. The expositors observe the encouragement this offers: God is able to do "exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think" (Ephesians 3:20); the believer, coming with small requests, may receive far greater gifts; the God who heals the lame gives beyond the measure of our prayers. The witnesses note the contrast between the man's narrow expectation and God's large purpose: he looked only for a little help to endure his affliction; God meant to remove it altogether — a picture of how God's grace surpasses our low expectations, giving salvation where we sought only relief. The expositors draw the lesson against unbelief and small asking: the man's careful seeking of mere maintenance, supposing his case hopeless, is the mark of low expectation; yet God's grace exceeds it — teaching the believer to look for great things from a great God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the surpassing grace that gives more than we ask, comes with small requests yet finds large gifts, and trusts the God who anticipates and exceeds our desires. The verse thus records the beggar's request for alms — while God purposed his healing — teaching the surpassing, preventing grace of God, who gives more than we ask or expect.
4:
"And Peter, fastening his eyes upon him with John, said, Look on us." The witnesses note Peter's fixed gaze and his call to attention. Barnes notes "fastening his eyes" denotes "to look intently, or with fixed attention" — a characteristic word of Luke (Luke 4:20; Acts 6:15; 7:55); Peter looking earnestly at the man, no doubt under the impulse of the Spirit. Lightfoot illustrates the call "Look on us" from the Jewish idiom of a healer bidding the sick to look on him. The witnesses note Peter fixing his eyes on the man and bidding him "Look on us" — fixing the beggar's attention before the miracle. The witnesses note Peter's earnest gaze and call.

The witnesses note Peter's fixed gaze and his "Look on us" — engaging the man's attention before the cure.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's gaze and call to attention. The lesson the expositors draw is the earnest attention with which the apostle prepared the man for the miracle, under the impulse of the Spirit. The witnesses note Peter's intent look: "fastening his eyes upon him with John" — Barnes notes the word denotes a fixed, earnest gaze (a word Luke often uses), Peter looking intently at the man, no doubt moved by the Spirit to mark him out for the cure; the earnest gaze a token of the apostle's spiritual discernment and purpose. The expositors observe the call: "Look on us" — Peter bids the man fix his attention on the apostles, to engage his expectation and prepare him for what was to come; Lightfoot illustrates the idiom from Jewish healers who bade the sick look on them. The witnesses press the purpose of fixing the man's attention: the miracle was not to be done carelessly or unobserved, but with the man's full attention engaged — that he might be a conscious participant, his faith and expectation drawn out, and the cure manifestly connected with the apostles' word in Christ's name. The expositors draw the lesson on the Spirit's impulse: Peter acted not at random, but under the moving of the Spirit, who marked out this man for healing; the apostle's earnest gaze the outward sign of the inward prompting — the miracle a work of the Spirit, directed to its appointed object. The witnesses note the engagement of the man before the cure: by bidding him "look on us," Peter drew the man's expectation toward them, preparing his heart to receive — the attention engaged, the faith stirred (the man expecting, v.5), the ground prepared for the word of power. The expositors observe the contrast with the man's expectation: he looked, expecting alms; Peter's gaze and call drew him toward a far greater gift, fixing his attention on the instruments through whom Christ would heal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the earnest attention of the apostle, the Spirit's impulse directing the miracle, and the engaging of the man's expectation before the cure. The verse thus records Peter's gaze and call — fastening his eyes, "Look on us" — teaching the earnest attention with which the apostle, moved by the Spirit, prepared the man for the miracle.
5:
"And he gave heed unto them, expecting to receive something of them." The witnesses note the man's eager attention and his expectation of alms. Clarke explains the custom: "it was a constant custom for all who entered the temple to carry money... to give to the treasury, or to the poor"; hence the gate the best place for alms. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes he "held his eyes right on Peter and John with great eagerness, expecting to receive something" — taking Peter's invitation "as a promise of a large gift." The witnesses note the man giving heed, fixing his eyes on the apostles, hoping for a generous alms. The witnesses note the man's expectant attention.

The witnesses note the man giving heed — eagerly expecting alms, hoping for a generous gift.

So the witnesses make the verse the man's expectant attention. The lesson the expositors draw is the man's eager but mistaken expectation, looking for alms when God purposed a far greater gift. The witnesses note his response to Peter's call: "he gave heed unto them" — fixing his eyes on the apostles, attending eagerly to them; Robertson notes he held his gaze right on them with great eagerness. The expositors observe the ground of his eagerness: "expecting to receive something of them" — he took Peter's "Look on us" as the promise of a generous alms; Clarke notes the custom of carrying money into the temple for the treasury or the poor, so that the beggar naturally hoped the apostles, bidding him look on them, were about to give him a substantial gift. The witnesses press the man's mistaken expectation: his hope was fixed on money, on alms — the most he could imagine; he expected something, but only a coin, never dreaming of the healing God purposed. The expositors draw the lesson on the limits of human expectation: the man's eager hope reached no higher than alms; he looked for the small gift, ignorant of the great one; a picture of how men, even when expectant, often look for far less than God means to give. The witnesses observe the providence in his eager attention: his expectation, though mistaken in its object, fixed his attention on the apostles, preparing him to receive — the eager looking the disposition through which the greater gift would come. The expositors note the contrast soon to appear: he expected silver and gold, the lowest gift; he received health and strength, a gift beyond price — the disproportion between his hope and God's bounty about to be displayed (v.6). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the man's eager but low expectation, and is taught to look higher than alms — to expect from God the great gifts of grace, not merely the small relief the flesh imagines. The verse thus records the man's expectant attention — giving heed, hoping for alms — teaching his eager but mistaken expectation, and the contrast between his low hope and the great gift God purposed.
6:
"Then Peter said, Silver and gold have I none; but such as I have give I thee: In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth rise up and walk." The witnesses note the apostles' poverty and the power in the name of Christ. Gill notes the apostles had no money — "silver and gold have I none" — having only what was put into the common stock; and that no money might be carried in a purse into the temple. Lightfoot confirms the rule against bringing money bound up into the temple. The witnesses note the great exchange: Peter has no silver, but gives what is infinitely better — healing in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth; the power not in the apostle but in the name. The witnesses note the cure commanded in Christ's name.

The witnesses note Peter's word: no silver, but healing in the name of Jesus — the power in the name, not the apostle.

So the witnesses make the verse the healing in the name of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the poverty of the apostles, the surpassing gift they had to give, and the power that lay in the name of Jesus, not in themselves. The witnesses note the apostles' poverty: "Silver and gold have I none" — Peter had no money to give; the apostles were poor, possessing nothing but the common stock of the church (Gill notes), and (Lightfoot adds) money might not even be carried into the temple. The expositors observe the great exchange: "but such as I have give I thee" — Peter, lacking silver, gives what is infinitely more precious; not a coin to relieve the man's poverty, but healing to remove his affliction; the gift surpassing all the alms the man had hoped for. The witnesses press the source of the power: "In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth rise up and walk" — the cure was wrought not by Peter's power, but in and by the name of Jesus; the name standing for the person, authority, and power of Christ; Peter the instrument, Christ the healer. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of the name: the apostles worked their miracles not in their own strength, but in the name of Jesus — by his authority and power; the name of Christ the source of healing, the apostle only the channel; so that all the glory belonged to Christ, not to Peter (as Peter would insist, v.12). The witnesses note the boldness of the command: Peter bids the lifelong cripple "rise up and walk" — a command impossible to obey by nature, but made possible by the power of the name; the word of faith laying hold of Christ's power for the helpless man. The expositors observe the contrast the verse sets: the world's wealth (silver and gold) the apostles lacked, but they had the riches of Christ's power; the Church, poor in earthly goods, rich in spiritual gifts — the treasure not of money but of the gospel and the name of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the apostles' poverty and their surpassing gift, the power in the name of Jesus, and the lesson that the Church's treasure is not silver but the name and power of Christ. The verse thus records the healing in Christ's name — no silver, but "rise up and walk" in the name of Jesus — teaching the apostles' poverty, the surpassing gift, and the power that lay in the name of Christ.
7:
"And he took him by the right hand, and lifted him up: and immediately his feet and ankle bones received strength." The witnesses note the helping hand and the instantaneous cure. Barnes draws a beautiful illustration from the helping hand: "this may be employed as an illustration of the manner of God when he commands men to repent and believe; he does not leave them alone; he extends help, and aids their efforts... his hand is stretched out, and his power exerted to impart strength and grace." On the cure, Barnes notes "the fact that strength was immediately imparted... was a full and clear proof of miraculous power." The witnesses note Peter taking the man's hand and the instant strengthening of his feet and ankles. The witnesses note the helping hand and the immediate miracle.

The witnesses note the helping hand and the instant cure — strength given immediately to the long-lame feet.

So the witnesses make the verse the helping hand and the instantaneous cure. The lesson the expositors draw is the union of the apostle's help with the man's effort, the picture it gives of grace, and the proof of the miracle in its suddenness. The witnesses note the action: "he took him by the right hand, and lifted him up" — Peter, having commanded the man to rise, took his hand and helped him up; not leaving him to struggle alone, but extending aid, encouraging the effort. The expositors draw out Barnes's beautiful illustration: this is a picture of God's manner when he commands men to repent and believe — he does not leave them to themselves, but extends help, aids their efforts; when the sinner, feeling his weakness, trembles, God's hand is stretched out to impart strength and grace; the command joined to the enabling, the duty to the help. The witnesses press the union of command and power: Peter commanded the man to rise (v.6), then took his hand to lift him — the word and the help together; and the strength came, not from the man's own effort nor from Peter's lifting, but from the power of Christ working through the apostle's hand and word. The expositors note the instantaneous cure: "immediately his feet and ankle bones received strength" — the strengthening was instant, the lifelong-lame feet and ankles at once made firm; Barnes notes this immediacy was the clear proof of miraculous power, for no natural healing is instantaneous. The witnesses observe the completeness of the cure: the feet and ankle bones, weak from birth, received full strength at once — not a gradual mending, but a sudden, perfect restoration; the bones, muscles, and sinews instantly made whole and strong. The expositors draw the lesson on the proof of the miracle: the suddenness and completeness of the cure, witnessed by all, was undeniable evidence of the power of Christ; no imposture or natural recovery could account for the instant strengthening of feet lame for forty years. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the helping hand that aids the commanded effort (a picture of grace), and the instant, complete cure that proved the power of Christ's name. The verse thus records the helping hand and the immediate cure — Peter lifting him, his feet at once made strong — teaching the union of command and help, the picture of grace, and the proof of the miracle in its suddenness.
8:
"And he leaping up stood, and walked, and entered with them into the temple, walking, and leaping, and praising God." The witnesses note the man's joyful response and the proof it gave. Barnes notes the leaping "was a natural expression of joy, and a striking fulfillment of the prophecy in Isaiah 35:6, Then shall the lame man leap as an hart"; and presses the genuineness — "the act of walking is one acquired by long practice; children learn slowly," yet this man, lame from birth, walked at once, "clear proof of a miracle." The witnesses note the healed man leaping, standing, walking, and entering the temple, praising God — the immediate, joyful exercise of his new strength. The witnesses note the man's joyful walking and praise.

The witnesses note the healed man leaping and praising God — his joyful walking the proof and fruit of the miracle.

So the witnesses make the verse the man's joyful response. The lesson the expositors draw is the natural overflow of joy in the healed man, the proof of the miracle in his immediate walking, and the fulfilment of prophecy. The witnesses note the burst of activity: "leaping up stood, and walked, and entered with them into the temple, walking, and leaping, and praising God" — the man, healed, sprang up, stood firm, walked, and leaped, going into the temple with the apostles, his whole frame in joyful motion. The expositors observe the fulfilment of prophecy Barnes marks: the leaping fulfilled Isaiah 35:6, "then shall the lame man leap as an hart" — the prophetic picture of the Messianic age, when the lame would leap, realized in this man; the miracle a sign of the kingdom Christ had brought. The witnesses press the proof of the miracle in his immediate walking: Barnes notes that walking is a skill acquired by long practice — children learn it slowly; yet this man, lame from birth and never having walked, walked at once, firmly and even leaped — a clear proof of a miracle, for no natural cure could give both strength and the instant skill of walking. The expositors draw the lesson on the completeness of Christ's healing: not only were the man's feet strengthened, but he could at once use them perfectly — walk, leap, run; the cure complete, the power and the skill given together; Christ's work whole and entire. The witnesses note the joyful praise: "praising God" — the man's first use of his new strength was to praise God; his joy overflowing in thanksgiving, recognizing the hand of God in his cure; the fitting response to so great a mercy. The expositors observe the example of the healed man: he did not merely enjoy his cure, but praised God for it, and entered the temple (the house of God) to worship — the healed soul turning to thanksgiving and worship; a pattern for all who have received mercy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the joyful response of the healed man, the proof of the miracle in his immediate walking, the fulfilment of prophecy, and the praise that should follow every mercy received. The verse thus records the man's joyful walking and praise — leaping, walking, praising God — teaching the overflow of joy, the proof of the miracle, the fulfilment of Isaiah, and the thanksgiving that becomes the healed.
9:
"And all the people saw him walking and praising God:" The witnesses note the public witness of the miracle. Barnes lists the particulars that put the miracle beyond any appearance of imposture: the man had been afflicted from a child, known to all; he was no impostor, for he had occupied the public place too long to be undetected; the apostles had no prior agreement with him, never having seen him; and the man himself was convinced of his cure. The witnesses note that all the people — accustomed to see the man begging — now saw him walking and praising God; the miracle witnessed by the whole multitude, its reality beyond dispute. The witnesses note the public attestation of the cure.

The witnesses note all the people seeing the healed man walk and praise God — the miracle publicly and undeniably attested.

So the witnesses make the verse the public attestation of the miracle. The lesson the expositors draw is the undeniable, public character of the cure, witnessed by all and beyond any suspicion of fraud. The witnesses note the public witness: "all the people saw him walking and praising God" — the multitude in the temple, accustomed to see the man lying and begging, now saw him walking and praising; the miracle done not in secret but before the eyes of all, its reality open to every observer. The expositors press the particulars Barnes lists that put the miracle beyond any appearance of imposture: first, the man had been lame from a child, his condition known to all the people; second, he was no impostor, for had he feigned lameness, he could not have escaped detection in so public a place over so many years; third, the apostles had no prior collusion with him, having never seen him before, so there was no agreement to deceive; and fourth, the man himself was convinced of his cure, his joy unfeigned. The witnesses draw the lesson on the evidence of the miracle: every circumstance combined to establish its truth — the lifelong, public lameness, the absence of collusion, the instant and complete cure, the man's own conviction, and the witness of the whole multitude; no fact could be further from the appearance of fraud. The expositors observe the providence in the publicity: God wrought the miracle openly, before the people, that it might be an undeniable testimony to the power of Christ and a foundation for the gospel Peter would preach; the public cure the occasion and the proof of the public sermon. The witnesses note the contrast the people beheld: the man they had so often passed by, lying helpless, now walking and praising — the change so great, so evident, that none could doubt; the miracle its own witness. The expositors draw the lesson on the confirmation of the gospel by such signs: the miracles of the apostles, done openly and beyond dispute, confirmed their testimony to Christ; the works attesting the word. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the public, undeniable attestation of the miracle, and the providence that wrought it openly to confirm the gospel. The verse thus records the public witness of the cure — all the people saw him walking and praising God — teaching the undeniable, public character of the miracle, beyond all suspicion of fraud, and its confirmation of the gospel.
10:
"And they knew that it was he which sat for alms at the Beautiful gate of the temple: and they were filled with wonder and amazement at that which had happened unto him." The witnesses note the people's recognition and amazement. Gill stresses the certainty of their recognition: "they knew him to be that beggar that sat daily and asked alms... it was a clear and indisputable point with them." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "they began to perceive" (inchoative imperfect) and "were filled" (effective aorist) with wonder. The witnesses note the people recognizing the healed man as the well-known beggar, and being filled with wonder at his cure. The witnesses note the recognition and amazement.

The witnesses note the people recognizing the beggar and being filled with wonder at his healing.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's recognition and amazement. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty of the recognition, which established the miracle, and the wonder it stirred, which prepared for the sermon. The witnesses note the recognition: "they knew that it was he which sat for alms at the Beautiful gate" — Gill stresses it was a clear and indisputable point; the people knew the man well, having seen him beg there daily for years, and now recognized the same man walking and praising; no mistake or doubt of identity. The expositors press the importance of this for the miracle's attestation: the people's certain recognition of the healed man as the lifelong beggar removed all possibility of fraud or mistaken identity; it was unmistakably the same man, his cure undeniable. The witnesses note the effect: "they were filled with wonder and amazement at that which had happened" — Robertson marks the inchoative imperfect (they began to perceive) and the effective aorist (were filled); the people, recognizing the man and seeing his cure, were overwhelmed with astonishment. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence in this amazement: the wonder stirred by the miracle prepared the people to hear; their astonishment drew them together (the next verse), and made them attentive to Peter's word — the miracle the occasion and the door for the sermon. The witnesses observe the right and the limited response: the people were filled with wonder — a fitting response to so great a work; yet wonder alone is not faith; the amazement must be directed (as Peter would direct it) from the marvel to its Author, from the miracle to the Christ whose power wrought it. The expositors note the danger Peter would guard against: the people, amazed, might ascribe the cure to the apostles (v.12); the wonder must be turned to the glory of Christ, not the men. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the certain recognition that established the miracle, the wonder that prepared the people, and the need to direct amazement at God's works to the glory of their Author. The verse thus records the people's recognition and amazement — knowing the beggar, filled with wonder — teaching the certainty that established the miracle and the wonder that prepared the way for the gospel.
11:
"And as the lame man which was healed held Peter and John, all the people ran together unto them in the porch that is called Solomon's, greatly wondering." The witnesses note the gathering of the amazed multitude in Solomon's porch. Lightfoot discusses the location of "Solomon's porch" — whether the eastern porch strictly, or the whole Court of the Gentiles, so called from Solomon. Gill and the witnesses note the healed man clinging to the apostles in gratitude and attachment, and the people running together in wonder. The witnesses note the multitude gathering, the man holding the apostles, the crowd assembled for the sermon in Solomon's porch. The witnesses note the gathered, wondering crowd.

The witnesses note the amazed crowd running together in Solomon's porch — the gathering that occasioned Peter's sermon.

So the witnesses make the verse the gathering of the multitude. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that gathered the wondering crowd, giving Peter the occasion to preach Christ. The witnesses note the man's attachment: "the lame man which was healed held Peter and John" — clinging to the apostles in gratitude and joy, keeping close to his benefactors; the healed man's devotion to those through whom Christ had healed him. The expositors observe the gathering: "all the people ran together unto them in the porch that is called Solomon's, greatly wondering" — the amazed multitude, drawn by the miracle, ran together to the apostles in Solomon's porch (Lightfoot discusses the location — the eastern porch, or the whole Court of the Gentiles); the crowd assembling in wonder. The witnesses press the providence in this gathering: the miracle drew the people together, and their wonder made them eager to hear; the assembled, attentive multitude the very congregation Peter needed for his sermon — the cure providing both the occasion and the audience for the preaching of Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the use of God's works to gather hearers: the miracle was not an end in itself, but the means of gathering the people to hear the gospel; God's wonders draw attention, that the word may be preached and souls won. The witnesses note the fitness of the place: Solomon's porch, a great colonnade of the temple, a place of public concourse and teaching (where Christ himself had taught, John 10:23); the gathered crowd in a place fit for the sermon to come. The expositors observe the man's continued presence: the healed man, holding the apostles, stood as a living witness before the crowd — the visible proof of the miracle, lending power to Peter's words; the cured man the text, as it were, of the sermon. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that gathered the wondering crowd, the use of God's works to draw hearers, and the healed man's grateful attachment to those through whom Christ had blessed him. The verse thus records the gathering of the multitude in Solomon's porch — the man holding the apostles, the people running together — teaching the providence that gathered the hearers and gave Peter the occasion to preach Christ.
12:
"And when Peter saw it, he answered unto the people, Ye men of Israel, why marvel ye at this? or why look ye so earnestly on us, as though by our own power or holiness we had made this man to walk?" The witnesses note Peter's deflecting of the glory from himself to Christ. Chrysostom observes the boldness of this sermon, the miracle having aroused the hearers; and notes the wisdom of Peter's opening: "nothing is so advantageous, and so likely to pacify the hearers, as to say nothing about one's self of an honorable nature, but on the contrary to obviate all surmise of wishing to do so" — Peter rejecting the glory the people would give him. The witnesses note Peter disclaiming his own power and holiness, turning the people from the apostles to the true Author. The witnesses note Peter deflecting the glory.

The witnesses note Peter disclaiming his own power — turning the people's wonder from the apostles to Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's deflecting of the glory. The lesson the expositors draw is the humility of the apostle, who refused the glory of the miracle and turned the people to its true Author. The witnesses note Peter's question: "why marvel ye at this? or why look ye so earnestly on us, as though by our own power or holiness we had made this man to walk?" — the people, amazed, were fixing their eyes on the apostles as the doers of the miracle; and Peter at once disclaims any power or holiness of his own as the cause, turning their wonder away from himself and John. The expositors observe the humility this displays: Peter, who might have basked in the people's admiration, refuses it, denying that the cure was wrought by his own power (as if he had strength to heal) or his own holiness (as if his merit had earned it); he will not steal the glory due to Christ. The witnesses press Chrysostom's note on the wisdom of this opening: nothing so conciliates hearers as to disclaim self-glory; Peter, by rejecting the honor the people would give him, both wins their attention and clears the way to give the glory to Christ; the humble disavowal the door to the gospel. The expositors draw the lesson on the right use of God's works: the apostle is but the instrument; the power and the glory are God's; and the faithful servant turns all admiration from himself to his Lord — "not unto us, O LORD, but unto thy name give glory" (Psalm 115:1). The witnesses note the danger Peter guards against: that the people, in their wonder, should ascribe the cure to the men, exalting the apostles and missing the Christ; Peter forestalls this, that the miracle might lead them not to admire him but to believe in Jesus. The expositors observe the contrast with false teachers and workers, who claim glory for themselves: Peter's humility the mark of the true servant, who seeks not his own honor but his Master's. The witnesses draw the application: the believer learns from Peter to disclaim all glory in the work of God, to turn admiration from the instrument to the Author, and to point men from the servant to the Lord. The verse thus records Peter's deflecting of the glory — not by our power or holiness — teaching the humility of the apostle, who refused the honor of the miracle and turned the people to Christ, its true Author.
13:
"The God of Abraham, and of Isaac, and of Jacob, the God of our fathers, hath glorified his Son Jesus; whom ye delivered up, and denied him in the presence of Pilate, when he was determined to let him go." The witnesses note Peter's grounding in the God of the fathers and the charge of denying Christ. Gill notes Peter retains these Old Testament titles of God "to distinguish him from the gods of the Gentiles," and to show "they were not setters forth of another, or a strange God, but believed in the same God their forefathers did." Barnes stresses the importance of showing it was "the same God who had done this that had been acknowledged by their fathers." The witnesses note Peter ascribing the miracle to the God of the fathers, who has glorified Jesus — whom the people denied before Pilate. The witnesses note the God of the fathers glorifying Jesus.

The witnesses note Peter's appeal: the God of the fathers has glorified Jesus — whom the people denied before Pilate.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's grounding in the God of the fathers and the charge of denial. The lesson the expositors draw is that the same God who covenanted with the fathers has glorified Jesus, whom the people sinfully rejected. The witnesses note the divine name Peter invokes: "The God of Abraham, and of Isaac, and of Jacob, the God of our fathers" — Gill notes Peter retains these Old Testament titles to distinguish the true God from the idols of the heathen, and to show that he and the apostles preached no strange God, but the same God the fathers worshipped; the gospel no new religion, but the fulfilment of the faith of Israel. The expositors press the importance Barnes marks: Peter establishes that it was the very God acknowledged by their fathers who had wrought this miracle and glorified Jesus; the continuity of the gospel with the covenant, the God of Abraham being the God who raised and exalted Christ. The witnesses note the great affirmation: this God "hath glorified his Son Jesus" — by raising him from the dead, exalting him to heaven, and now working this miracle in his name; God has set his seal of glory on the Jesus the people despised. The expositors draw the lesson on the vindication of Christ: the same God of the fathers, whom the people professed to worship, has glorified the very Jesus they rejected — so that in rejecting Jesus, they opposed their own God; the glorifying of Christ God's reversal of their condemnation. The witnesses press the charge Peter brings: "whom ye delivered up, and denied him in the presence of Pilate, when he was determined to let him go" — the people delivered Christ to death and denied him, even when Pilate, the heathen judge, was minded to release him; their guilt the deeper, for they pressed for the death of the innocent whom even Pilate would have freed. The expositors observe the contrast Peter draws: God glorified Jesus; the people denied him — the divine honor set against the human rejection; the same Jesus exalted by God and despised by men. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the continuity of the gospel with the faith of the fathers, the glorifying of the rejected Christ by the God of Abraham, and the guilt of denying the One whom God has honored. The verse thus records Peter's grounding in the God of the fathers — who has glorified Jesus, whom the people denied — teaching the continuity of the gospel, the vindication of Christ, and the guilt of his rejection.
14:
"But ye denied the Holy One and the Just, and desired a murderer to be granted unto you;" The witnesses note the aggravated charge of preferring Barabbas to Christ. Gill expounds the titles: Christ "the Holy One" both in his divine and human nature and the fountain of holiness; "the Just" or righteous in person and office, who wrought a righteousness for his people — these titles set against "the unjust charges of his enemies." Barnes notes "the Just" denotes innocent, free from crime, uncondemned even before his judges. The witnesses note the dreadful exchange: the people denied the Holy and Just One, and demanded a murderer (Barabbas) in his place. The witnesses note the preferring of a murderer to the Holy One.

The witnesses note the aggravated charge: denying the Holy and Just One, preferring a murderer in his place.

So the witnesses make the verse the aggravated charge of preferring Barabbas. The lesson the expositors draw is the enormity of the people's sin in rejecting the Holy and Just One and choosing a murderer. The witnesses note the titles Peter gives Christ: "the Holy One and the Just" — Gill expounds: "the Holy One," holy in both natures and the fountain of holiness to his people; "the Just," righteous in person and office, who wrought a righteousness for his own; Barnes adds that "the Just" denotes innocent, free from crime, uncondemned even by his judges. The expositors press the contrast these titles draw: the people denied the very One who was holy and righteous — the sinless, innocent Christ; their rejection not of a guilty man but of the Holy and Just One, the enormity of their sin heightened by his perfect innocence. The witnesses note the dreadful exchange: "and desired a murderer to be granted unto you" — they not only denied Christ, but demanded Barabbas, a murderer, in his place; preferring a man of blood to the Author of life, choosing the guilty over the innocent (Luke 23:18-25). The expositors draw the lesson on the perversity of the choice: the people's preferring a murderer to the Holy One is the measure of their blindness and their sin — the most dreadful exchange, the worst preferred to the best, the guilty to the guiltless, the destroyer to the Saviour. The witnesses observe the aggravation of guilt: to deny Christ was sin; to deny the Holy and Just One was greater; to prefer a murderer to him was greater still — Peter heaping up the charge, that the people might feel the depth of their wickedness and be brought to repentance. The expositors note the purpose of the charge: not to crush the people in despair, but to convict them of their sin, that they might repent (as Peter would call them, v.19); the conviction the path to the pardon. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the enormity of rejecting the Holy and Just One, the perversity of preferring sin to the Saviour, and the conviction of sin that prepares for repentance. The verse thus records the aggravated charge — denying the Holy and Just, desiring a murderer — teaching the enormity of the people's sin in rejecting the innocent Christ and choosing a murderer in his place.
15:
"And killed the Prince of life, whom God hath raised from the dead; whereof we are witnesses." The witnesses note the paradox of killing the Prince of life and the witness of the resurrection. Gill expounds "the Prince of life" (or Author of life): the living God, who has life in himself, the author of spiritual resurrection and procurer of eternal life; "now this Lord of life and glory they crucified." Barnes notes the word "Prince" denotes a leader, captain, or author (as in Hebrews 2:10), one who is the cause or source — here the Author of life. The witnesses note the dreadful paradox: the people killed the Author of life, whom God raised; and the apostles witness his resurrection. The witnesses note the killing of the Prince of life.

The witnesses note the paradox: the people killed the Author of life — whom God raised, the apostles witnessing.

So the witnesses make the verse the paradox of killing the Prince of life and the witness of the resurrection. The lesson the expositors draw is the dreadful contradiction of slaying the Author of life, the divine reversal in the resurrection, and the apostolic witness. The witnesses note the title: "the Prince of life" — or, Barnes notes, the Author, Leader, or Captain of life (as in Hebrews 2:10, "the captain of their salvation"); the source and giver of life. Gill expounds it richly: Christ the living God, having life in himself, the author of the spiritual resurrection from death in sin, and the procurer and giver of eternal life; the very fountain of life. The expositors press the dreadful paradox: "and killed the Prince of life" — the people put to death the Author of life himself; the creatures slaying their Creator, the dying killing the source of life; the most monstrous contradiction, that life's Author should be slain by those he gave life to. The witnesses note the laying of the death to their charge: though the Romans crucified Christ, the death is charged on the people, for it was at their request, instigation, and earnest solicitation that Pilate condemned him (Gill notes); their guilt real, though Pilate's hand wrought it. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine reversal: "whom God hath raised from the dead" — the very One they killed, God raised; the resurrection God's overturning of their crime, his vindication of the Prince of life, his declaration that death could not hold the Author of life; what man slew, God raised. The witnesses press the apostolic witness: "whereof we are witnesses" — Peter and the apostles testify to the resurrection, having seen the risen Lord; the central fact of the gospel attested by eyewitnesses, the foundation of the preaching. The expositors observe the contrast the verse heaps up: the people killed him, God raised him; they slew the Prince of life, but life could not be held by death; the resurrection the answer to the murder, the vindication of the slain. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marvels at the paradox of the slain Author of life, rejoices in the resurrection that reversed the crime, and rests on the apostolic witness to the risen Christ. The verse thus records the killing of the Prince of life and his resurrection — whom God raised, the apostles witnessing — teaching the dreadful paradox of slaying life's Author, the divine reversal in the resurrection, and the witness on which the gospel rests.
16:
"And his name through faith in his name hath made this man strong, whom ye see and know: yea, the faith which is by him hath given him this perfect soundness in the presence of you all." The witnesses note the power of the name and the faith that is by Christ. Barnes explains "his name" as standing for Jesus himself — "it was done by his authority and power"; not that there was efficacy in the mere name, but that the cure was by the authority of Christ. He notes the faith "by him" — the faith which Peter and John had in Jesus. The witnesses note Peter ascribing the cure to the name (the power) of Christ, through faith — and even the faith itself as the gift "which is by him." The witnesses note the name and faith as the means of the cure.

The witnesses note the cure ascribed to Christ's name through faith — even the faith itself a gift by him.

So the witnesses make the verse the power of the name and the faith that is by Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the cure was wrought by the name (the power and authority) of Christ, through faith — and that even the faith is his gift. The witnesses note the ascription: "his name through faith in his name hath made this man strong" — Peter attributes the cure not to himself but to the name of Jesus; and Barnes clarifies that "his name" stands for Jesus himself, the cure done by his authority and power, not by any efficacy in the bare name as a charm; the name the expression of the person, power, and authority of Christ. The expositors press the means: "through faith in his name" — the cure came through faith; whether the faith of the apostles who used the name, or the faith of the healed man, or both; faith the appointed channel through which Christ's power was applied. The witnesses note the careful guarding against superstition: it was not the magical utterance of a name that healed, but the power of Christ, laid hold of by faith; the name and the faith the means, Christ the power. The expositors draw the lesson on the source of the faith itself: "the faith which is by him" — Peter ascribes even the faith to Christ; the faith that laid hold of the cure was itself the gift of Christ, "by him" — so that not only the power but the very faith was of Christ; the believer's faith his gift, the channel he himself supplies (Ephesians 2:8). The witnesses press the completeness of the cure: "this perfect soundness in the presence of you all" — the man made perfectly whole, completely sound, before the eyes of all; the cure entire, public, and undeniable. The expositors observe the humility maintained: Peter, even in explaining the cure, gives all to Christ — the name, the power, and the faith; nothing to himself; the glory wholly the Lord's. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests the power of all blessing in the name of Christ, lays hold of it by faith, and acknowledges even his faith as Christ's gift; the cure of the lame man a picture of salvation by the name of Jesus through faith. The verse thus records the power of the name and the faith by Christ — his name through faith making the man whole — teaching that the cure was by Christ's authority through faith, and that even the faith is his gift.
17:
"And now, brethren, I wot that through ignorance ye did it, as did also your rulers." The witnesses note Peter's tenderness and the mitigation of ignorance. Barnes marks the tenderness: though they had committed so enormous a crime, Peter still addresses them as "brethren," showing "the tenderness of his heart"; this verse "a remarkable instance of tenderness in appealing to sinners" — for "men are melted to contrition, not by reproaches, but by love." On "through ignorance," Barnes notes Peter charitably allows that they and their rulers acted in ignorance — a mitigation, though not a full excuse. The witnesses note Peter, having charged their sin, now tenderly allowing that they acted in ignorance. The witnesses note the tenderness and the ignorance.

The witnesses note Peter's tenderness — calling them "brethren" and allowing they acted in ignorance.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's tender appeal and the mitigation of ignorance. The lesson the expositors draw is the tenderness with which the apostle, having convicted the people of sin, now seeks to win them, and the place of ignorance as a mitigation. The witnesses note the tenderness Barnes marks: "and now, brethren" — Peter, having charged them with the dreadful crime of killing the Prince of life, yet addresses them as "brethren," with the tenderness of his heart; not reproaching them harshly, but appealing with love. The expositors press the wisdom of this tenderness: men are melted to contrition not by reproaches but by love; Peter, having stated and proved their wickedness, now seeks to bring them to repentance by kindness, not by crushing them — the tender appeal the way to the heart. The witnesses note the mitigation Peter allows: "I wot that through ignorance ye did it, as did also your rulers" — Peter charitably grants that the people and their rulers acted in ignorance, not knowing fully whom they crucified (as Christ himself had prayed, "they know not what they do," Luke 23:34; cf. 1 Corinthians 2:8). The expositors draw the lesson on ignorance as a mitigation, not a full excuse: their ignorance lessened their guilt — they did not fully know that Jesus was the Messiah and the Lord of glory; yet it did not wholly excuse them, for their ignorance was itself culpable, springing from unbelief and the rejection of the evidence; hence Peter still calls them to repentance (v.19). The witnesses observe the balance Peter strikes: he neither excuses their sin entirely (they must repent) nor crushes them in despair (their ignorance leaves room for mercy); the charge convicts, the tenderness invites, the mitigation opens hope. The expositors note the pastoral skill: Peter, the model preacher, convicts of sin yet offers hope, charges guilt yet appeals with love, leaving the door of repentance open even to the murderers of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer learns from Peter to appeal to sinners with tenderness, to convict yet to invite, and to mark that ignorance mitigates but does not excuse, leaving the call to repentance. The verse thus records Peter's tender appeal and the mitigation of ignorance — brethren, ye did it through ignorance — teaching the tenderness that wins the heart, and the place of ignorance as a mitigation that leaves room for the call to repent.
18:
"But those things, which God before had shewed by the mouth of all his prophets, that Christ should suffer, he hath so fulfilled." The witnesses note the suffering of Christ as the foretold and fulfilled purpose of God. Barnes notes "by the mouth of all his prophets" means "the prophets in general, or the prophecies as a whole," not that each individually predicted it; and "hath so fulfilled" — God "caused to be fulfilled in this manner; that is, by the rejection, denial, and wickedness of the rulers." The witnesses note that the sufferings of Christ, which the people sinfully caused, were yet the fulfilment of what God had foretold by all the prophets — the divine purpose accomplished even through human sin. The witnesses note the foretold suffering fulfilled.

The witnesses note the suffering of Christ foretold by all the prophets — fulfilled even through the people's sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the suffering of Christ as the foretold purpose of God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sufferings of Christ, though caused by the people's sin, were the fulfilment of God's purpose foretold by all the prophets. The witnesses note the foretelling: "those things, which God before had shewed by the mouth of all his prophets, that Christ should suffer" — the sufferings of the Messiah were no surprise, but foretold throughout the Old Testament; Barnes notes "all his prophets" means the prophets in general, the prophecies as a whole, testifying that Christ must suffer (Isaiah 53; Psalm 22; and many more). The expositors press the divine purpose: the suffering of Christ was God's appointed plan, declared beforehand by the prophets; the cross no accident or defeat, but the foreordained way of redemption, foretold and certain. The witnesses note the manner of fulfilment: "he hath so fulfilled" — Barnes notes God caused it to be fulfilled in this very manner, by the rejection and wickedness of the rulers; the people's sin the means by which the foretold suffering came to pass; God overruling their crime to accomplish his purpose. The expositors draw the lesson on the union of divine purpose and human sin: the people sinfully killed the Prince of life (v.15), yet in doing so they fulfilled what God had foretold; their guilt real, the divine purpose accomplished through it; the mystery of God's sovereignty overruling man's sin (as at Acts 2:23, "delivered by the determinate counsel of God... ye have crucified"). The witnesses observe the comfort and conviction Peter draws: comfort, that the cross was God's plan, not the failure of the Messiah; and conviction, that the people, though fulfilling prophecy, were yet guilty, and must repent. The expositors note the use Peter makes of this: the fulfilment of prophecy in Christ's suffering both proves Jesus to be the Messiah (the suffering Christ foretold) and prepares the call to repentance — for the foretold suffering was for their redemption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the suffering of Christ as God's foretold purpose, marvels at the sovereignty that fulfilled it through human sin, and is moved to repentance by the cross that was for him. The verse thus records the suffering of Christ foretold and fulfilled — by all the prophets, accomplished even through sin — teaching the divine purpose of the cross, the union of God's sovereignty and man's guilt, and the proof of Jesus as the suffering Messiah.
19:
"Repent ye therefore, and be converted, that your sins may be blotted out, when the times of refreshing shall come from the presence of the Lord;" The witnesses note the call to repentance and the promise of refreshing. Gill notes the Ethiopic adds "and be baptized." Lightfoot examines the Greek of "when the times of refreshing shall come" (hopōs an elthōsi), arguing it should rather be rendered "that the times of refreshing may come" — the repentance unto the blotting out of sins, that the refreshing may follow. The witnesses note the double call — repent and be converted (turned) — the promise that sins be blotted out, and the times of refreshing from the Lord's presence. The witnesses note the call to repentance and conversion.

The witnesses note the call: repent and turn, that sins be blotted out and times of refreshing come from the Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to repentance and the promise of refreshing. The lesson the expositors draw is that repentance and conversion lead to the blotting out of sins and the times of refreshing from God. The witnesses note the double call: "Repent ye therefore, and be converted" — repentance, the turning from sin in heart and mind; and conversion, the turning to God; the two joined, the inward change and the outward turning (some texts adding "and be baptized," Gill notes). The expositors press the "therefore": because Christ has suffered according to prophecy (v.18), and because they have sinned in rejecting him (yet in ignorance, v.17), they are called to repent — the conviction of sin issuing in the call to repentance, the gospel offer following the charge. The witnesses note the first promise: "that your sins may be blotted out" — the forgiveness of sins, the guilt cancelled, the record erased; the blotting out a vivid figure of full pardon, the sins wiped away as a debt cancelled or a writing erased (Isaiah 43:25; Colossians 2:14). The expositors draw the lesson on the connection of repentance and pardon: repentance and conversion are the way to the blotting out of sins — not as the meritorious cause (Christ's blood alone procures pardon), but as the appointed means and condition; the penitent and converted soul receiving the forgiveness Christ purchased. The witnesses note the second promise: "the times of refreshing shall come from the presence of the Lord" — and Lightfoot's argument that the Greek is better rendered "that the times of refreshing may come"; the refreshing the seasons of spiritual comfort, revival, and blessing that follow repentance. The expositors weigh what "the times of refreshing" are: many take them of the gospel comfort and revival enjoyed now, the refreshing of the soul by pardon and the Spirit; others extend them to the future blessedness, the rest and refreshing at Christ's coming (the next verses speaking of his return). The witnesses observe that both may be embraced: repentance brings present refreshing in the comfort of forgiveness and the Spirit, and looks forward to the fuller refreshing at the restitution of all things. The witnesses draw the application: the believer heeds the call to repent and turn, seeks the blotting out of his sins, and looks for the times of refreshing from the Lord's presence. The verse thus records the call to repentance and the promise of refreshing — repent, be converted, that sins be blotted out — teaching that repentance and conversion lead to pardon and the seasons of refreshing from God.
20:
"And he shall send Jesus Christ, which before was preached unto you:" The witnesses note the sending of Christ and its meaning. Gill takes this not of Christ's first or second coming, but of his being sent "in the ministration of the word, and by his Spirit, to the comfort of their souls" — Christ coming spiritually to the penitent through the gospel. Barnes takes it more broadly of the whole economy under which God would send Christ to teach, redeem, save, and judge. The witnesses note the promise that God will send Jesus Christ — whether in spiritual comfort through the word, or in his ultimate coming; the One already preached to them now offered. The witnesses note the sending of the preached Christ.

The witnesses note the promise that God will send Jesus Christ — coming to the penitent by his word and Spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of Christ to the penitent. The lesson the expositors draw is that to those who repent, God sends Christ — in the comfort of his word and Spirit, and in the hope of his coming. The witnesses note the promise: "he shall send Jesus Christ, which before was preached unto you" — God's response to repentance is to send Christ; the One already preached to them now given for their comfort and salvation. The expositors weigh the meaning of "send." Gill takes it not of Christ's first coming (past) nor of his second (which might terrify the guilty), but of his spiritual coming — Christ sent to the penitent in the ministration of the word and by his Spirit, to the comfort of their souls; the risen Lord coming spiritually to those who turn to him, refreshing them with his presence. Barnes takes it more broadly, of the whole economy under which God sends Christ — to teach, redeem, save, and judge; the dispensation in which Christ is given for all the needs of his people. The witnesses observe that the sending answers the repentance of the previous verse: to those who repent and are converted, God sends Christ — the pardon, the refreshing, the presence of the Lord, all in the Christ he sends. The expositors draw the lesson on the reward of repentance: the penitent is not left desolate, but receives Christ — sent by the Father to comfort, save, and dwell with him; the turning to God met by the sending of his Son. The witnesses note the continuity with the preaching: "which before was preached unto you" — the same Jesus they had heard preached (by Christ himself, by the apostles) is the one God sends; the gospel offer renewed, the rejected Christ still offered to the penitent. The expositors observe the grace in this: the very Christ they had denied and killed (v.14-15) is the one God now offers to send to them — the rejected Saviour still held out to his rejecters, if they will repent; the riches of grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer, repenting, receives the Christ God sends — in the comfort of the word and Spirit now, and in the hope of his coming; the penitent's reward the presence of the Lord. The verse thus records the sending of Christ to the penitent — he shall send Jesus Christ — teaching that God sends Christ to those who repent, in the comfort of his word and Spirit, the rejected Saviour still offered to his people.
21:
"Whom the heaven must receive until the times of restitution of all things, which God hath spoken by the mouth of all his holy prophets since the world began." The witnesses note Christ's heavenly session and the times of restitution. Barnes explains "whom the heaven must receive" against the Jewish expectation that the Messiah would reign on earth forever (John 12:34): it was fit and proper that Christ should ascend, to direct the affairs of the universe for the church's welfare (John 16:7); the apostles' evidence being that they saw him ascend (Acts 1:9). The witnesses note Christ retained in heaven until "the times of restitution of all things" — the restoration foretold by the prophets, at his coming. The witnesses note the heavenly session until the restitution.

The witnesses note Christ received in heaven until the restitution of all things — foretold by all the prophets.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's heavenly session and the times of restitution. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ must remain in heaven until his coming, when all things foretold by the prophets will be restored. The witnesses note the heavenly session: "whom the heaven must receive" — Christ, having ascended, must be received and retained in heaven; Barnes notes this was stated against the Jewish expectation that the Messiah would reign visibly on earth forever (John 12:34), and to establish that Jesus, though ascended, was truly the Messiah. The expositors press the fitness of his remaining in heaven: it was proper and expedient that Christ should ascend and remain there — to send the Spirit (John 16:7), to intercede as High Priest, and to direct the affairs of the universe for the welfare of the church (Ephesians 1:22); his heavenly session no absence from his people, but their advantage. The witnesses note the term: "until the times of restitution of all things" — Christ to remain in heaven until the restitution, the restoration of all things at his coming; the renewal of creation, the setting right of all that sin has marred, the consummation of the kingdom (the "regeneration," Matthew 19:28; the "new heavens and new earth," 2 Peter 3:13). The expositors weigh "the restitution of all things": the great renewal at Christ's return, when all things are restored and made new — the resurrection, the judgment, the new creation; the full and final setting-right of the world, foretold by all the holy prophets since the world began. The witnesses observe the grounding in prophecy: "which God hath spoken by the mouth of all his holy prophets since the world began" — the restitution no novel idea, but foretold throughout the Scriptures, from the first promise onward; the whole prophetic witness pointing to the final restoration. The expositors draw the lesson on the present and the future: Christ now in heaven, reigning and interceding; and the restitution to come at his return — the believer living between the ascension and the restitution, in the age of the Spirit, awaiting the renewal of all things. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the heavenly session of Christ (his intercession and rule), and looks for the times of restitution at his coming, foretold by all the prophets. The verse thus records Christ's heavenly session and the times of restitution — received in heaven until all things are restored — teaching the present reign of the ascended Christ and the future renewal foretold by the prophets.
22:
"For Moses truly said unto the fathers, A prophet shall the Lord your God raise up unto you of your brethren, like unto me; him shall ye hear in all things whatsoever he shall say unto you." The witnesses note Peter's appeal to Moses' prophecy of the great Prophet. Barnes stresses the weight of Moses' authority — "absolute and final" among the Jews; the object being to show that Moses himself foretold the Messiah, and that following Christ was no departure from Moses. He notes "a prophet" in Deuteronomy 18 used "in a large sense, to denote one who should infallibly guide and direct the nation in its religious affairs." The witnesses note Moses' prophecy (Deuteronomy 18:15-19) of a prophet like himself, whom the people must hear in all things — applied to Christ. The witnesses note the prophet like Moses.

The witnesses note Moses' prophecy of a Prophet like himself — applied to Christ, whom the people must hear in all things.

So the witnesses make the verse Moses' prophecy of the great Prophet. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses himself foretold the Messiah, the Prophet like himself, whom all must hear. The witnesses note the appeal to Moses: "Moses truly said unto the fathers, A prophet shall the Lord your God raise up unto you of your brethren, like unto me" — Peter cites Deuteronomy 18:15-19, where Moses foretold that God would raise up a prophet like himself, of the people, to whom they must hearken. The expositors press the weight Barnes marks: Moses' authority was absolute and final among the Jews; and Peter, by showing that Moses himself foretold the Messiah, proves that to follow Christ is no departure from Moses, but the very thing Moses commanded — the law itself pointing to Christ. The witnesses note the description "like unto me": the promised prophet would be like Moses — a mediator between God and the people, a lawgiver, a leader, a worker of signs; and Christ fulfilled this likeness surpassingly, the true Prophet greater than Moses (Hebrews 3:3), the Mediator of a better covenant. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ as the great Prophet: Jesus is the Prophet Moses foretold, raised up of his brethren (truly man, of Israel), to declare God's will fully and finally; the One in whom prophecy culminates, the Word of God incarnate. The witnesses note the command: "him shall ye hear in all things whatsoever he shall say unto you" — the people are bound to hear and obey the great Prophet in all things; his word final and authoritative, to be received without exception; the same charge God gave at the transfiguration, "this is my beloved Son... hear ye him" (Matthew 17:5). The expositors observe the force for Peter's hearers: Moses, whom they revered, commanded them to hear the Prophet like himself; and that Prophet is Christ, whom they had rejected — so in rejecting Christ, they disobeyed Moses; the appeal turning their own reverence for Moses into a call to hear Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer hears Christ the great Prophet in all things, receives his word as final and authoritative, and marks that to follow Christ is to obey Moses, who foretold and commanded it. The verse thus records Moses' prophecy of the Prophet like himself — him shall ye hear — teaching that Moses foretold Christ the great Prophet, whom all must hear in all things.
23:
"And it shall come to pass, that every soul, which will not hear that prophet, shall be destroyed from among the people." The witnesses note the solemn warning against rejecting the great Prophet. Barnes notes "hear that prophet" means "obey his instructions"; he that does not obey the Prophet "refuses to obey God." On "shall be destroyed," he notes the Hebrew of Deuteronomy 18:19 is "I will require it of him" (hold him answerable, punish him), the Septuagint "I will take vengeance"; Peter gives the sense — the rejecter cut off. The witnesses note the dreadful penalty: every soul who will not hear (obey) Christ the Prophet shall be destroyed, cut off from the people of God. The witnesses note the penalty of rejecting Christ.

The witnesses note the solemn warning: whoever will not hear the great Prophet shall be destroyed from the people.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn warning against rejecting Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the dreadful penalty of refusing to hear the great Prophet — destruction, cutting off from the people of God. The witnesses note the warning Moses gave (Deuteronomy 18:19), which Peter cites: "every soul, which will not hear that prophet, shall be destroyed from among the people" — the refusal to hear the Prophet visited with destruction; the penalty for rejecting Christ. The expositors press the meaning of "hear" that Barnes marks: to "hear that prophet" means to obey his instructions, to receive and submit to his word; and he that does not hear refuses to obey — not a mere failure to listen, but a rejection of the Prophet's authority, a refusal to submit to Christ. The witnesses note the penalty: "shall be destroyed from among the people" — Barnes notes the Hebrew is "I will require it of him" (hold him answerable, punish him), the Septuagint "I will take vengeance"; Peter gives the sense — the rejecter destroyed, cut off from the people of God, excluded from the covenant and its blessings. The expositors draw the lesson on the consequence of rejecting Christ: to refuse the great Prophet is to incur destruction; the one who will not hear Christ cuts himself off from God's people and God's salvation; the penalty fearful and final. The witnesses observe the force of the warning for Peter's hearers: Moses himself had warned that whoever rejected the Prophet would be destroyed; and they had rejected that Prophet (Christ); so they stood under the warning — yet, by repentance (v.19), might escape it; the warning a spur to repentance. The expositors note the solemnity Peter presses: the gospel is no light matter; to reject Christ is to incur destruction; the call to hear him is a call on which eternal life or death depends. The witnesses draw the lesson on the authority of Christ: the great Prophet must be heard and obeyed in all things; to refuse him is fatal; the believer is to submit wholly to his word, on pain of destruction. The witnesses draw the application: the believer hears and obeys Christ the Prophet, marks the dreadful penalty of rejecting him, and is moved by the warning to repentance and submission. The verse thus records the warning against rejecting Christ — destroyed from the people — teaching the dreadful penalty of refusing to hear the great Prophet, and the call to obey him on pain of destruction.
24:
"Yea, and all the prophets from Samuel and those that follow after, as many as have spoken, have likewise foretold of these days." The witnesses note the whole prophetic witness to the gospel age. Gill notes Samuel was reckoned by the Jews "the master of the prophets" and "the first prophet" after Moses, the head of the prophetic line; so Peter names him as next to Moses, the prophets from Samuel onward foretelling these days. Barnes notes "all the prophets" means the prophets in general, not that each made a distinct prediction. The witnesses note that all the prophets, from Samuel onward, foretold the days of the Messiah — the whole prophetic witness converging on Christ and the gospel. The witnesses note the prophets foretelling these days.

The witnesses note all the prophets from Samuel onward foretelling the gospel days — the whole witness converging on Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the whole prophetic witness to the gospel age. The lesson the expositors draw is that not Moses only, but all the prophets foretold the days of Christ — the entire prophetic testimony converging on the Messiah and the gospel. The witnesses note the sweep of the appeal: "all the prophets from Samuel and those that follow after, as many as have spoken, have likewise foretold of these days" — Peter, having cited Moses, now appeals to the whole line of the prophets, from Samuel onward, as witnesses to the days now come. The expositors observe Gill's note on Samuel: reckoned by the Jews the master of the prophets, the first in the prophetic line after Moses (few prophets coming between), the head of the schools of the prophets; so Peter names him as the next great witness after Moses, the prophets from his time foretelling these days. The witnesses note Barnes's clarification that "all the prophets" means the prophets in general, the prophetic witness as a whole — not that every individual prophet made a distinct prediction of these very days, but that the body of prophecy, taken together, foretold the Messiah and his age. The expositors draw the lesson on the unity of the prophetic testimony: the whole Old Testament, Moses and all the prophets, bears witness to Christ; the gospel age the fulfilment of the entire prophetic word; "the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy" (Revelation 19:10). The witnesses press the force for Peter's hearers: not one prophet only, but all, foretold these days; so in receiving Christ, they would be embracing the fulfilment of their whole prophetic heritage; in rejecting him, they opposed all the prophets they revered. The expositors observe the privilege of "these days": the days of the Messiah, foretold and longed for by all the prophets, were now come — the days the prophets desired to see (Matthew 13:17); the hearers living in the fulfilment of the ages of prophecy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whole prophetic witness to Christ, sees the gospel age as the fulfilment of all prophecy, and counts it a privilege to live in the days the prophets foretold. The verse thus records the whole prophetic witness — all the prophets from Samuel foretold these days — teaching the unity of the prophetic testimony to Christ and the privilege of the gospel age foretold by all.
25:
"Ye are the children of the prophets, and of the covenant which God made with our fathers, saying unto Abraham, And in thy seed shall all the kindreds of the earth be blessed." The witnesses note the privilege of the hearers as heirs of the prophets and the covenant. Gill notes they were "the children of the prophets" — descended from Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (called prophets) — to whom belonged the prophecies and promises; "heirs of them." Barnes takes "children of the prophets" as their disciples and followers, who should therefore listen to them; and notes "the covenant" — they were "sons of the covenant," heirs of the promise to Abraham. The witnesses note the hearers as heirs of the prophetic promises and the Abrahamic covenant, in whose seed all nations are blessed. The witnesses note the privilege of the covenant heirs.

The witnesses note the hearers as heirs of the prophets and the Abrahamic covenant — the blessing of all nations in the seed.

So the witnesses make the verse the privilege of the hearers as covenant heirs. The lesson the expositors draw is the high privilege of the Jews as heirs of the prophets and the covenant, and the universal blessing promised in Abraham's seed. The witnesses note the first privilege: "ye are the children of the prophets" — Gill takes this as descended from Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (called prophets), to whom the prophecies and promises belonged, and the hearers their heirs; Barnes takes it as their disciples and followers, bound therefore to heed them.
