The expositors observe the second privilege: "and of the covenant which God made with our fathers" — the hearers were "sons of the covenant," heirs of the covenant made with Abraham and the fathers; the covenant of promise theirs by descent and inheritance. The witnesses note the covenant promise cited: "And in thy seed shall all the kindreds of the earth be blessed" (Genesis 22:18) — the great Abrahamic promise, that in Abraham's seed all the families of the earth would be blessed; the seed being Christ (Galatians 3:16), in whom the blessing comes to all nations. The expositors draw the lesson on the privilege and the responsibility: the hearers, as heirs of the prophets and the covenant, had the first claim on the promised blessing — the gospel coming first to them (v.26); their privilege great, their responsibility to receive the Christ in whom the covenant is fulfilled. The witnesses press the universal reach of the promise: "all the kindreds of the earth" — the blessing of Abraham's seed not for the Jews only, but for all nations; the gospel, springing from the covenant, going out to bless the whole earth; the Abrahamic promise the foundation of the world-wide mission. The expositors observe the force for the hearers: they, as covenant heirs, should be the first to embrace the seed in whom the blessing comes; to reject Christ would be to forfeit their own inheritance, to refuse the blessing their covenant promised. The witnesses note the grace toward them: though they had rejected Christ, they are reminded of their covenant privilege and offered the blessing first — the riches of grace to the heirs who had despised their inheritance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the covenant privilege of the hearers, the universal blessing in Abraham's seed (Christ), and the gospel's reach to all nations springing from the covenant. The verse thus records the hearers as covenant heirs — children of the prophets and the covenant — teaching their high privilege and the universal blessing promised in Abraham's seed, fulfilled in Christ.
26:
"Unto you first God, having raised up his Son Jesus, sent him to bless you, in turning away every one of you from his iniquities." The witnesses note the gospel offered first to the Jews and the nature of the blessing. Barnes notes "unto you first" — the gospel to be preached first to the Jews, beginning at Jerusalem (Luke 24:47); "having raised up" used as in v.22 (God raising up and commissioning his Son to teach), not of the resurrection; and the blessing as "turning away every one of you from his iniquities." Gill allows "raised up" of either the incarnation or the resurrection. The witnesses note the gospel sent first to the Jews, the blessing of Abraham's seed being the turning of each from his sins. The witnesses note the blessing of turning from iniquity.

The witnesses note the gospel offered first to the Jews — the blessing being the turning of each one from his iniquities.

So the witnesses make the verse the gospel offered first to the Jews and the nature of the blessing. The lesson the expositors draw is that God sent Christ first to bless the Jews, and that the blessing is the turning of each from his sins. The witnesses note the priority: "Unto you first God... sent him to bless you" — the gospel and its blessing offered first to the Jews; Barnes notes the gospel was to be preached first at Jerusalem and to Israel (Luke 24:47; Romans 1:16, "to the Jew first"); the covenant heirs given the first offer of the blessing in Abraham's seed. The expositors observe the meaning of "having raised up his Son Jesus": Barnes takes it (as in v.22) not of the resurrection but of God's raising up and commissioning his Son to teach and bless — sending him forth; Gill allows it of either the incarnation (raising him up, manifesting him) or the resurrection and exaltation; in either case, God sent his Son to bless. The witnesses note the nature of the blessing: "to bless you, in turning away every one of you from his iniquities" — the blessing of the covenant, the great gift Christ brings, is the turning of each one from his sins; not earthly prosperity, not national glory, but deliverance from sin — repentance, sanctification, the turning from iniquity. The expositors draw the lesson on the chief blessing of the gospel: the greatest blessing Christ brings is to turn men from their sins — to deliver them from the power and practice of iniquity; salvation is, first of all, salvation from sin (Matthew 1:21, "he shall save his people from their sins"); the blessing of Abraham fulfilled in the turning of souls from evil. The witnesses press the individuality: "every one of you from his iniquities" — the blessing personal, each one to be turned from his own sins; the gospel calling each individual to forsake his particular iniquities; no general blessing without personal repentance. The expositors observe the grace toward the hearers: God sent his Son to bless the very people who had rejected him — to turn them from the sins (including the sin of rejecting Christ) for which Peter had charged them; the riches of grace, blessing the guilty by turning them from their guilt. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the gospel offered first to the Jews, the blessing of turning from sin as the chief gift of Christ, and the personal call to forsake his own iniquities. The verse thus records the gospel offered first to the Jews and the blessing of turning from iniquity — sent to bless you, in turning every one from his sins — teaching the priority of the offer, and the chief blessing of the gospel in deliverance from sin, closing Peter's sermon with the call to forsake iniquity.
